title: The BWRX-300 SMR produces 5x less power in a reactor building that is only 20% smaller than it’s precursor, the 1600MW ESBWR. | Chris Keefer MD
text:
The BWRX-300 SMR produces 5x less power in a reactor building that is only 20% smaller than it’s precursor, the 1600MW ESBWR. This diagram below is a side by side, to scale comparison of the 1600MW economic simplified boiling water reactor (ESBWR) and the GE Vernova Hitachi Nuclear Energy BWRX-300. The ESBWR reactor building is 36.5m in diameter vs. 34.4m for the BWRX-300. The ESBWR is 69.3m high compared to 63.8m for the BWRX-300. Ontario is investing in this American reactor project under the delusion that we are pioneering a new special class of nuclear reactor technology, the mythical "small modular reactor." It has even been elevated to the state of a “nation building project” by the Mark Carney federal government. The yet to be modularized BWRX-300 is not in fact an SMR. It is a Large, Small output, not modular reactor. We CANDU better than this Canada. Large proven Canadian reactor technology that takes advantage of economies of scale to meet our growing needs. Timothy Hodgson Doug Ford Our NRCan | Notre RNCan
As strong an advocate as you are for the Canadian nuclear sector, I wish you'd tone down the anti-SMR rhetoric. It's unhelpful. Yes, CANDU is great. Yes, a new CANDU design will hopefully be ready in a few short years. Yes, we should support it as a nation. But, fact is, it wasn't in play for DNNP, the wave of pro-SMR sentiment has created the conditions where we can build new-nuclear (both smaller and larger) again, and OPG and the province, made pragmatic choices necessary for Canada to remain a nuclear leader rather than a follower during this wave of nuclear optimism. The DNNP ship has sailed, and she's a beautiful ship, even if she's smaller than other ships. As for the SMR moniker, it's just that, a moniker. It was, and in many cases remains, a convenient way of describing new(er) reactor designs. The criteria for being 'an SMR' are pretty artificial, and aren't necessarily stuck to religiously, but that doesn't really matter, and obviously we "canna' change the laws of physics" (I've always wanted to say that in a post). Moreover, early nuclear wasn't called small, yet compared to today was definitely that. It doesn't matter. Hopefully we all want Canadian nuclear to succeed, so lets focus on the positives.
Interesting paradox: the BWRX-300 SMR delivers about five times less power than its predecessor, the 1,600 MWe ESBWR, yet its reactor building is only about 20% smaller (34.4 m vs. 36.5 m in diameter; 63.8 m vs. 69.3 m in height). At first glance, this seems counterintuitive. But the point of SMRs is not to shrink the building in exact proportion to power, it is to simplify, modularize, and de-risk construction. The BWRX-300 trades volumetric efficiency for: - streamlined passive safety features, - shorter schedules and easier replication, - lower financing risk and faster time to revenue. In nuclear, cost and competitiveness are often driven less by sheer size than by complexity, standardization, and buildability. That is why the BWRX-300 can still be an attractive option for new markets, even if its building footprint is not radically smaller than that of a gigawatt-scale plant.
…“mythical small modular reactor”…. I love it. I suspect that turn of phrase may become part of the nuclear history lexicon when this era of nuclear renaissance ends and we collectively come to our senses. I hope I’m proven wrong, but it seems so obvious that the world needs large capacity nuclear to slow and eventually eliminate the CO2 waste streams from power plants.
The worry for me is the issue of this build being first. There's never been a case where a first build has gone smoothly without delays and cost overruns. If that same problem happens here, it might make the stated rationale a bit less compelling.
It could be a huge mistake to make a nuclear project one of the major project targets. The risk is nuclear culture, standards and regulations. Experts all believe that the CNSC is an excellent regulator however in some none technical spheres there is the appearance of poltics. It is a huge risk for a Goverment major project office to be able to override the CNSCs pursuit of safe.
To be fair, ESBWR on paper was one of the most amazing reactors ever so comparing anything to it will sound like a downgrade and comparing BWRX300 to an as built BWR in the fleet may be more insightful. Too bad ESBWR was not built, maybe had some manufacturability issues or something as it's a real mystery to me why ESBWR wasn't built.
The BWRX-300 is generate about 300 MWe, with the first-of-a-kind project estimated at around US 5.6 billion. In comparison, conventional large PWRs or BWRs (typically 1,000–1,600 MWe) have capital costs in the range of US 2–4 billion per unit. Despite this, no new BWR orders have been placed globally since Fukushima, except in Canada and Poland. It’s also important to note that a natural circulation ( I have serious concerns at this point) BWR has no prior commercial or operational experience to build upon, and its economic advantages remain uncertain. Personally, I find it unusual that such a design is moving forward — but I sincerely hope the project succeeds and that I am proven wrong.
